evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
Entity of type FAQPage has no @id — cross-page interconnection blocked
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~14 FAQ pairs
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
Specific human voice vs committee 5/10 — Some personality peeks through (the Dave/Doodle line), but most of the piece reverts to marketing-committee cadence.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 6/10 — The Wanderlog road-trip concession and 'Dave ghosting' show real takes, but most comparisons read like neutral bullet points.
Unexpected phrasings 6/10 — A few fresh lines (thumbs-up emoji three days late, group chat dying on trip four) are outnumbered by boilerplate comparison phrasing.
Specific citations 6/10 — Only one concrete price ($29.99/year Wanderlog Pro) and a date. Competitor pricing, user counts, and specific features are vague.
Quotable sentences 6/10 — The Dave/Doodle line is genuinely quotable; most other sentences are feature-list prose.
Intro earns attention 4/10 — The opening is a duplicated nav crumb plus a generic TL;DR; the real hook (Dave ghosting Doodle) is buried paragraphs in.

answer_extractability 5/10 — The primary keyword demands a clean, extractable answer stating which app wins and why, ideally within the first 60 words of the body. Instead, the TL;DR hedges with 'most apps excel in one area' and the 'Quick answer' block is written in first-person narrative form, not a structured answer an AI can lift verbatim. A Perplexity snippet needs a declarative sentence naming the winner with clear criteria before any nuance is introduced.

specificity 5/10 — The post names the right apps and gives one concrete price ($29.99/year for Wanderlog Pro), but repeatedly invokes '[google-travel-trends]' as a citation while describing the data in vague, unquantified language ('steady rise,' 'especially for trips among friends'). Multiple section headers — 'What does each app actually do for the organiser?' — resolve into bullet lists of adjectives ('strong,' 'flexible,' 'overkill') rather than concrete feature counts, user numbers, or named limitations. The 4–10 friends sizing note is the only user-scale figure in the piece.
